does: This work presents Sea Limited's deployment strategy and practical experience integrating OpenAI's Codex into their engineering workflow at scale across multiple teams in Asia. The paper documents how the company adopted an AI-native approach to software development, examining concrete implementation decisions, team organization, and measured outcomes from rolling out code generation capabilities across their organization. It provides empirical observations on productivity impacts, engineering velocity changes, and organizational adaptations needed when incorporating large language models into the development process at enterprise scale.
connects: This work builds on emerging research and practice in AI-assisted software engineering, extending earlier explorations of code generation models like Codex with organizational and practical deployment perspectives. It connects to parallel efforts in the machine learning community studying how neural code generation affects developer productivity, code quality, and team dynamics. The paper responds to growing industry interest in understanding how foundation models trained on code can be operationalized in real engineering contexts, moving beyond academic benchmarks to examine the human and organizational factors that determine successful adoption of generative AI tools in production software development environments.
enables: This deployment experience and analysis enables future work on scaling AI-native development practices across large distributed teams and geographic regions. It provides a foundation for studying long-term productivity effects, knowledge transfer implications, and how organizations should restructure development practices around AI code assistance. The work points toward research on team dynamics in AI-augmented development, optimal ways to integrate code generation into CI/CD pipelines and code review processes, and how different development cultures and skill levels interact with generative AI tools. It also enables comparative studies on regional differences in adoption patterns and effectiveness across different engineering domains.
